coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as emerging members of the carbon nanomaterial family with ultra-small sizes and unique physicochemical properties, which has attracted a great deal of attention in energy conversion and storage, especially in rechargeable batteries in recent years. Although CDs have shown remarkable potential in different batteries, the mechanism of its role for solving common problems in batteries is lacking. In this review, we first clarify the constitutive relationship between the preparation, modification and electrochemical properties of CDs and provide strategies for structural design. Notably, we categorize the role of CDs in batteries from a new perspective, detailing the mechanism that CDs function in different electrochemical properties. Finally, we provide several issues facing CDs in battery applications and directions for further research. This review is expected to serve as an insightful guidance for scientists who work with the fascinating material of CDs and to stimulate further exploration in energy storage.

head.ce:title: Deciphering carbon dots in a new perspective from structural engineering to mechanisms in batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fascinating properties of CDs demonstrate outstanding potential in batteries. This paper provides a new perspective on the application of CDs in batteries, clarifying the conformational relationship between the synthesis, modulation and electrochemistry of CDs. Specifically, the roles of CDs in batteries are categorized and their mechanisms of action are elucidated.

Carbon dots (CDs), as emerging members of the carbon nanomaterial family with ultra-small sizes and unique physicochemical properties, which has attracted a great deal of attention in energy conversion and storage, especially in rechargeable batteries in recent years. Although CDs have shown remarkable potential in different batteries, the mechanism of its role for solving common problems in batteries is lacking. In this review, we first clarify the constitutive relationship between the preparation, modification and electrochemical properties of CDs and provide strategies for structural design. Notably, we categorize the role of CDs in batteries from a new perspective, detailing the mechanism that CDs function in different electrochemical properties. Finally, we provide several issues facing CDs in battery applications and directions for further research. This review is expected to serve as an insightful guidance for scientists who work with the fascinating material of CDs and to stimulate further exploration in energy storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the worldwide development of science and technology, there is an increasing demand for energy, which has put forward higher requirements for next-generation energy storage technologies [1,2]. It has become a hot topic to promote energy industry's green and efficient transformation. In recent years, carbon materials (e.g. graphite, carbon nanotubes, graphene, porous carbon, etc.) play a crucial role in electrode material fabrication because of their advantages, such as lower cost, larger surface area, superior electrical conductivity and natural self-expanding ability. For example, graphite has a honeycomb structure that provides high thermal and mechanical stability, resulting in excellent electrical conductivity and prolonged cycling stability, making it the primary anode material for batteries [3,4]. Carbon nanotubes have a unique one-dimensional nanostructure with a higher specific surface area relative to graphite, excellent electronic conductivity, and outstanding chemical stability and flexibility [5]. Graphene, in addition to having a large specific surface area, has a large number of defects that provide abundant adsorption sites for greater capacity [6]. The porous structure of the connected porous carbon can not only efficiently transport electrons and ions in the electrode, but also exhibit small volume changes during inserting and extracting of lithium ions [7].

Although carbon materials have shown exciting properties for energy storage, some inherent problems still deserve to be highlighted. For example, graphite has low cost but comparatively poor material electrochemical properties. Graphene, carbon nanotubes, and porous carbon show outstanding performance, but with a higher cost. On the contrary, CDs, a new generation of materials, show extraordinary potential for battery applications [8–10]. Specifically, CDs possess the following advantages in battery applications [11,12]. CDs are easy to synthesize and the raw materials are widely available, which greatly reduces the preparation cost [13,14]. The material is easy to modify, and the modulation of structural and surface properties can be achieved by simple strategies [15–17]. CDs have a quantum state with a drastically reduced size, leading to quantum effects that greatly shorten the diffusion path and effectively increase the ion diffusion, electron transfer and charge transfer rates [18]. CDs can provide electrode materials with larger specific surface areas, active sites, or build functional groups that facilitate ion adsorption, which can easily lead to enhanced ion storage [19]. More importantly, due to the fact that CDs can regulate the ion nucleation process or induce crystal surface growth, the dendrite problem of the battery is effectively avoided [20]. With a series of fascinating properties of CDs, it is possible to achieve more electrochemical performance improvements in batteries and even realize new battery scenarios for applications [21].

Since CDs were first synthesized in 2004, most reviews have focused on its fluorescence properties. Only a few reviews have focused on its electrochemical properties. For instance, the applications of CDs in different batteries, and the structural and interfacial modifications of CDs in electrochemical energy sources were summarized by Hou et al. [22,23]. Xiong et al. described the application of CDs in supercapacitors and battery materials [24]. On the other hand, Lu et al. explored the synthetic methods, structural features and modification/functionalization strategies of CDs and summarized the CDs applications related to electrochemical energy, including electrocatalysis, batteries, and supercapacitors [25]. These excellent reviews can provide valuable insights and ideas for current research and facilitate the development of unique properties of CDs and their application in high-performance batteries. However, these reviews have only discussed the application of CDs in different batteries, or the summaries of the role of CDs in batteries are only partial and have not yet summarized the specific role of CDs in batteries in detail. Therefore, further in-depth integration and clarification of the mechanism of CDs in batteries is highly desired.

This review provides a new perspective and systematically summarizes the synthesis of CDs, and modification strategies that are beneficial to batteries. The roles of CDs in batteries are categorized and their mechanisms of action are elucidated (Scheme 1 ). As shown in Scheme 1, we first summarize the current definition of CDs and the four types of CDs (graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbon polymerization dots (CPDs)), and describe the two main preparation methods of CDs, “top-down” and “bottom-up”. The physicochemical properties of CDs are further elucidated and their suitable modification methods are provided to clarify the relationship between the modification strategy and the battery configuration. Then, the mechanisms of CDs in batteries are summarized into several types in terms of modulating material morphology, improving structural stability, regulating transport processes, enhancing ion storage capacity, controlling dendrite growth, inhibiting polysulfides, solid electrolyte interface modulation, and inhibiting parasitic reactions are elucidated in detail. Finally, the future prospects and challenges of CDs in battery applications are discussed.

CDs are an emerging type of nanoparticle, a generic term for nanoscale photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials that typically have a quasi-spherical morphology with a size smaller than ten nanometers. As shown in Fig. 1 , CDs can be classified into four categories based on differences in carbon cores: graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [26], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [27], carbon nanodots (CNDs) [28], and carbon-polymerized dots (CPDs) [23]. GQDs with π-conjugated single sheet and CQDs possess a spherical structure both exhibit quantum confinement and crystal structures. In the majority of cases, the precursors used to synthesize them are distinguished as CQDs and GQDs. Graphene-based materials are the main precursors of GQDs, while CQDs can be prepared by pyrolysis of other carbon nanomaterials or organic molecules with a crystalline structure at high temperatures. CNDs usually refer to quasi-spherical nanodots in which quantum confinement is lacking. They are called C-dots or carbon nanoclusters in some studies. CPDs are formed by the carbonization of polymer nanodots, including some incomplete carbonization with some polymer chain residues [23].

The controlled synthesis of CDs have been realized in recent years, providing a foundation for CDs' practical application in batteries. As shown in Fig. 2 , there are a large number of carbon source materials and synthesis methods that have been developed for the preparation of CDs. These available enrichment synthesis methods can be broadly categorized into two main groups, namely, “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods. The “top-down” approach is mainly based on the decomposition of larger carbon structures into smaller ones by chemical and physical means. The “bottom-up” approach is achieved by small aromatic molecules' stepwise chemical coupling or organic small molecules' pyrolysis, dehydration, and carbonization [29].

For the “top-down” approach, CDs are produced through a process of chemical or physical cutting of bulk carbon structures such as coal, bitumen, graphene and suspended carbon powder. Chemical oxidation [30], electrolysis [31], and laser irradiation [32] are the main methods used. These methods are realized by a stepwise dissociation procedure for cutting sp2-like carbon-based materials or bulk graphite-based into CDs. They usually require vigorous oxidation by strong acids (bases) in combination with strong oxidizing agents and hydrothermal or solvent-thermal cleavage under high temperature and pressure. CDs formed by “top-down” methods typically have a high degree of crystallinity and a distinctive structure. However, the above methods usually require expensive equipment or harmful chemical reagents, and with low yields of CDs. In addition, the prepared CDs had relatively monotonous surface chemical groups, while the purification process is complicated and time-consuming. This leads to significant difficulties in effectively regulating the physicochemical and electrochemical properties of CDs [14]. The combination of these factors has limited their widespread application (Table 1 ).

In contrast, the “bottom-up” method usually has a variety of carbon sources as precursors, such as polymers, organic small molecules. With optimizing solvents, changing carbon sources, adjusting reaction temperatures and other synthetic conditions, the CDs' physicochemical properties can be easily and precisely controlled. Different CDs with various nanostructures and chemical compositions can be fabricated. The “bottom-up” method generates CDs by polymerization under relatively mild conditions, usually by hydrothermal [33], microwave [34], solvent-thermal [35], microfluidic [36], electromagnetic induction heating [37], etc. The “bottom-up” approach involves precursors possessing polymerizable sites, comprising dehydratable groups or unsaturated bonds. Initially, the precursors undergo dehydration and intermolecular condensation for the formation of long chain polymers. After that, the aggregates form clusters. It experiences further cross-linking as the spatial distance between the chains in the cluster decreases. As a result, it becomes denser and more stable, giving rise to polymer dots. Some CPDs are produced during carbonization, and as carbonization proceeds, the core forms regions of microcrystalline carbon, resulting in CQDs, GQDs, or CNDs [25]. The purity of the CDs can be more accurately controlled by the use of well-chosen or designed precursors or solvents than by the “top-down” method. In addition, the method enables the simultaneous addition of heteroatoms during the initial carbonization process, resulting in homogeneous heteroatom doping and enriched surface functional groups. The size and surface state of the CDs can be achieved by adjusting the reaction time and temperature. The “bottom-up” method is simpler to prepare and easier to purify, and is more commonly used nowadays. It is more suitable for practical applications in the future.

There is no doubt that with the development of computer technology and artificial intelligence. It has become a new trend to guide the synthesis of CDs by machine learning methods [38,39]. Machine learning is utilized to determine the optimal synthesis conditions. For example, recently Wang et al. used machine learning algorithms to intelligently guide a novel multi-objective optimization strategy for hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs [40]. Utilizing a closed-loop approach to learn from finite and sparse data, it significantly shortens the research cycle and outperforms traditional trial-and-error methods. This lays the foundation for using machine learning-guided synthesis of CDs to develop new materials with multiple desired properties.

Up to now, although many methods have been reported for the preparation of CDs, there are still some problems in the large-scale CDs preparation. Nowadays, the strategies for the preparation of CDs are still dominated by energy-intensive pyrolysis, hydrothermal/solvothermal methods. The appearance of new and more efficient methods, such as microwave, ultrasound, and microfluidics, may lead to the acceleration of the development of industrial preparation and application of CDs. There is a need to develop new separation and purification techniques to isolate and purify CDs from the complex mixtures formed during synthesis [13]. The further development of CDs materials will require the struggle of more researchers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the actual application of materials, it is essential to first clarify the fundamental structure and properties of CDs. This understanding will facilitate informed design and selection processes in battery development. The basic properties of CDs are often focusing on several key points, including morphology, elemental composition, and defects. Usually, the most basic CDs have a standard spherical structure, which is mainly composed of carbon and oxygen elements, and may have different degrees of defects due to atomic doping or post-processing. After about two decades of research, the fundamental properties of CDs have been clarified, and this subsection describes the most basic and common basic physicochemical properties of CDs.

Most CDs have a similar morphology, although they are synthesized using different methods and precursors. They are usually with spherical structures of less than 100 nm. For example, Chen et al. prepared CDs by hydrothermal method using clover as a carbon source [41]. As presented in Fig. 3 a, the TEM image exhibits a uniform distribution of CDs. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the in-plane (100) lattice spacing of graphene. As shown in Fig. 3b, the size distribution ranges from 1.5 to 4.8 nm, in which the mean size is 2.77 nm. The morphological image of CDs by AFM showed a mean size of 5.2 nm (Fig. 3c). The size difference between TEM and AFM suggests that the CPDs that form the CDs have a core–shell structure. Naturally, in addition to the conventional spherical CDs, triangular CDs also exist. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, three-fold symmetric PG triangulogen was performed in solvothermal reactions at different reaction times [42]. The PG triangulogen has a unique structure as a raw material, featuring in a single molecule three highly reactive hydrogen atoms activated by three electron-donating hydroxyl groups in three interstitial positions, which is a key point in the synthesis of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 3e, elements C, O and N are the constituents of most CDs [10]. Surface groups and structures make an important contribution to the ability of CDs to be used as advanced electrode materials. For instance, for CDs, the amino groups on the surface contribute significantly to the pseudo-capacitance, and the large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups is essential in enhancing the wettability between the electrolyte and the electrode [23]. Moreover, the abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs can be coordinated with some metal ions, leading to particle size and morphology changes of the metal oxides, thus realizing the modulation of the metal oxide structure. When introduced together with different groups, it can realize the regulation of oil solubility over water solubility, so it can be used as a separator modifier and electrolyte additive.

Similar to other carbon crystalline materials, defects also exist within CDs. Raman spectroscopy is an effective way to verify the degree of structural ordering of carbon materials and is widely used as a means to scale the presence of defects in CDs. As shown in Fig. 3f, the Raman spectrum of CDs has two peaks corresponding to the D-band (1381 cm−1 for carbon associated with defects and disorder) and the G-band (1600 cm−1 for in-plane stretching vibrations in the sp2 domain) [41]. There are two main types of intrinsic defects in CDs, point defects (e.g., vacancies, dislocations, Stone-Wales defects) and line defects (e.g., crystal edges, interfaces). Usually, the electron-hole symmetry is broken due to the presence of point defects or facet defects [43]. Defects in CDs are a powerful and effective means of enhancing carbon electrode materials in batteries. The transport and storage of ions (Li+, K+, Na+) can be achieved through activation defects with higher adsorption energy and lower energy barriers. In addition, these defects provide abundant ion channels and surface area for electrolyte storage and diffusion, thus greatly increasing the battery capacity and charge/discharge rate [9,44]. Compared to the defective carbon of sp3 hybridization, the carbon atom of sp2 hybridization also has an important role in the battery. The backbone structure composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms has an extremely strong electrical conductivity, which significantly boosts the material's electrical conductivity [45]. Besides, the sp2 hybridized carbon nanostructures can provide carriers for long-range transport [46].

Over the past decade, the synthetic methods and properties of CDs have made significant progress. There have been many synthetic methods developed for green, environmentally friendly and bulk preparation of CDs. The carbon-rich natural wastes can be utilized in the most effective and direct way. The fundamental composition and structure of CDs are gradually clarified, and their formation mechanism has also been explained to a certain extent in a more reasonable way. This also provides a basis for the efficient application of CDs in battery materials. However, there are still some problems that need to be solved, such as the purity of the prepared CDs, and the relationship between the synthesized means and the structural composition need to be further clarified. Besides, the electrochemical properties of CDs need to be put into more investigation.

Actually, there are many influencing factors for the application of carbon materials in batteries, including the morphology of the material, the atoms it contains, and the interfacial properties can affect the final performance differences. The size of CDs can change with the means of synthesis method, temperature, etc. The change of the size can have many effects on the battery materials, including the diffusion length, the electronic structure, and the atomic configurations. Mixed-atom doping in the lattice of the most basic CDs that only contain the C and O elements can also change some of the material's properties, such as generating a large number of active sites, enlarging the interlayer distance, and improving the electronic conductivity. In addition, the surface functional groups of CDs can have a dramatic effect on interfacial and electrochemical properties. Amino groups can enhance the pseudo-capacitance, and hydroxyl and carboxyl groups help to improve the wettability between the electrode and the electrolyte. Surface-tailored functional groups can induce ion deposition processes in the battery. Therefore, it is extremely important to understand the modification strategies of CDs, and this section aims to clarify the modulation methods and approaches of CDs.

As the material size changes, it can make a difference to the battery material in many ways, including diffusion length, electronic structure, and atomic configuration. Kinetic activity can be enhanced by size reduction, but at the same time may lead and robust side reactions and poor thermodynamic stability [47]. Therefore, the practical application of materials requires that size effects be fully understood, as well as a full understanding of the controllability of material. Currently, size regulation of CDs is mainly dependent on precursor materials, temperature, reaction time, and post-purification.

Regulating the size of CDs can be well achieved by replacing the precursor or modulating the precursor ratio. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CDs were prepared by one-step pyrolysis by Cao et al. using ionic liquid [Bmim]Cl as the solvent and citric acid (CA) and o-phenylenediamine as the solutes [48]. As the CA content in the precursor decreases, a significant increase in the size of the obtained CDs occurs. Certainly, by replacing the CA in the precursor with pyrocatechol, CDs with larger size can be obtained. The reaction solvent, as an important factor in the reaction environment, also has an extremely important effect on the size of CDs. Xiong et al. obtained CDs with panchromatic emission by heating a mixture of o-phenylenediamine in four different solvents (H2SO4, formamide, ethanol, dimethylformamide) and L-glutamic acid (Fig. 4b) [35]. TEM images indicate that these CDs are well dispersed with sizes of about 1.8, 3.1, 5.2 and 7.6 nm, respectively. There is no doubt that the time and temperature of the reaction will also affect the size of the CDs to varying degrees. For example, Du et al. obtained a series of CDs with different sizes by one-step electrochemical oxidation of L-ascorbic acid at different reaction times [49]. Analyses by TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) showed that CDs mean particle size increased with reaction time. Cong et al. prepared CDs by different reaction temperatures and the prepared CDs decreased with increasing temperature [50].
Naturally, there are other influences in other synthesis methods, such as potentials in electrochemical synthesis methods that also have an effect on the size of the material.

Although controlling the precursor or reaction conditions can achieve some degree of size control, the CDs prepared directly usually have a wide range of size distributions. For this reason, using post-processing techniques can further realize the size control of CDs. Currently, purification techniques such as dialysis [51], ultrafiltration [52], column chromatography [53], and gel electrophoresis [54] can be utilized to achieve further control of CDs size. Yan et al. used o-PD and trimellitic acid (TMLA) as precursors to prepare a white luminescent CDs (WCDs) utilizing a one-step solvothermal method [55]. The WCDs were well dispersed but displayed a broad particle size distribution. Therefore, purification was necessary to achieve CDs size control and separation of WCDs mixtures. As shown in Fig. 4c, WCDs was purified and separated via column chromatography. After separation, four uniformly dispersed CDs were obtained with a narrower and more uniform particle size distribution. It is also possible to directly obtain CDs with uniform size distribution by growing them in some mesoporous materials. For example, Ortega-Liebana et al. used ordered mesoporous silica nanorods as limiting templates to prepare CDs by rapid pyrolysis (Fig. 4d) [56]. Rapidly thermal decomposition within the finite dimensions of their pores resulted in a highly homogeneous size distribution of the CDs, with an average size below 4 nm. As another example, Dong et al. utilized SAPO-20 molecular sieves as an effective template for the preparation of CDs, and SAPO-20 significantly enhances pyrrolidine, tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH), and zeolite backbone interactions, resulting in a promoted confinement effect and a homogeneous size distribution of CDs [57]. Along with more advanced characterization techniques, purification techniques, and the development of mesoporous materials, more and more means of size control will be possible.

Extensive experimental and theoretical research have shown that doping of heteroatoms in CDs can change some of the properties of the material, such as creating defects that increase the diffusion rate of ions. Or causing an increase in layer spacing while achieving better ion storage capacity. Carbon materials doped with heteroatoms can provide excellent cycle life, superior rate performance and higher capacity for batteries compared to undoped pure carbon materials [58]. The ease of achieving heteroatom doping is another excellent property of CDs. Depending on the number of heteroatoms introduced into CDs, they can be mainly classified into monoatomic doping and multi-atomic co-doping. Therefore, this subsection will detail two different types of doping to facilitate the efficient design of CDs.

For single-atom doping, it is subdivided into non-metal atom doping and metal atom doping. For non-metal atom doping, the commonly used doping elements are nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, etc. As Fig. 5 a shows, Yang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) by solvothermal method with HNO3, oPD [59]. HNO3 not only serves as a catalyst to accelerate the reaction rate, but also reacts easily with oPD via electrophilic reaction. HNO3 reacts with oPD mainly via nitration and introduces NO2 into it, and then these nitroxides are reduced to NH2. Among the intermediates, they can be protonated under strong acidic conditions, followed by oxidative polymerization. Carbonization at high pressure and temperature causing the formation of NCDs with conjugated aromatic skeletons. However, if the dose of HNO3 is too low, efficient conjugated aromatic π-systems and hydrogen bonding will not be established since NH2 is only partially protonated. It contributes to the π-conjugated systems with a pair of p-electrons in the NCDs. As the concentration of HNO3 increases, the ratio of pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen also rise, which facilitates the formation of NCDs characterized by more efficient conjugated aromatic π-system. For metal atom doping, it is sufficient to introduce metal ions into the synthesized precursor. Cu-CDs were prepared by Wu et al. through pyrolysis of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] at different temperatures, as shown in Fig. 5b [60]. On the one hand, it is difficult to carbonize Na2[Cu(EDTA)] to form CDs when it is below 250 °C. On the other hand, when the temperature is over 350 °C, the carbonization is too high to form CDs at the nanoscale, which leads to the formation of powders at the microscale. The Cu content of CDs decreases with increasing temperature. CuII can be easily reduced to Cu0 by carbon, and the higher pyrolysis temperature leads to the decrease of CuII content in Cu-CDs. Similarly, Tian et al. prepared Ni-CDs by hydrothermal treatment of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), nickel chloride hexahydrate, and ethylenediamine (EAN) at 180 °C [61]. It is noteworthy that the introduction of metal ions may realize the regulation of the electronegativity of CDs to some extent.

Single-atom doping creates strong feasibility conditions for the application of CDs in batteries. The low energy storage capacity and poor electrochemical activity of bare carbon materials based on the intrinsic storage mechanism hinder their further applications. Therefore, doping modification of heteroatoms is extremely important for them. For example, nitrogen atoms are more electronegative than carbon atoms and interact more strongly with metal ions. Extrinsic defects created by carbon surface doping with nitrogen can significantly increase electronic conductivity and reactivity. Doping of sulfur, which has a greater covalent radius compared to nitrogen, may dramatically expand carbon's interlayer distances. Sulfur accelerates the ion diffusion kinetics and at the same time can provide additional ion storage sites [62]. The doping of metal atoms facilitates the conversion of electrocatalytic polysulfides in lithium-sulfur batteries [17]. Therefore, single-atom doping of CDs is expected to show new vitality in battery energy storage materials.

Compared to single-atom doping, multi-atom co-doping can be synergistic or multifunctional, thus creating more possibilities for material applications. The polyatomic doped CDs are predominantly dual-atom doped. As shown in Fig. 5c, Zhang et al. used a hydrothermal method to prepare N, S co-doped CDs using α-lipoic acid, urea, and CA as sulfur, nitrogen, and carbon sources, respectively [63]. First dissolve sodium hydroxide using deionized water to obtain an alkaline solution. Then, a uniform yellow solution was formed by simultaneous addition of CA, α-lipoic acid and urea to the alkaline solution. The solution has been continuously hydrothermally heated at 230 °C for 18 h and dried by dialysis to obtain N, S-CDs. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 5d, bimetallic-doped Ce/Gd-CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method by Jing et al. [64]. In brief, dissolution of Ce(NO3)3-6H2O, oPD and Gd(NO3)3-6H2O in distilled water was performed while stirring continuously to fully dissolve. The brown powder obtained after heating at 200 °C followed by isolation and purification and freeze-drying was Ce/Gd-CDs. CDs except for the doping of similar non-metallic atoms, and mixed doping of metallic and non-metallic atoms can also be realized. As shown in Fig. 5e, Li et al. prepared N, Fe co-doped CDs using CA, DETA, and FeCl3-6H2O as carbon, nitrogen, and Fe sources, respectively [65]. CA can be passivated by DETA under hydrothermal conditions to form amide bonds, which helps to chelate Fe ions. Finally, after the polymerization and carbonation process, N, Fe-CDs possessing sp2 graphite structure were synthesized. The abundant presence of surface chemical groups makes them water-soluble.

So far, CDs have also enabled the doping of more elements. For instance, N, B, P-CDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of CA, phenylphosphonic acid (PPPA) and aminobenzeneboronic acid (ABBA) under specific conditions by Zhu et al. [66]. The development of multi-atom co-doping of CDs has deepened the possibilities for their application in batteries. More and more researches prove that multi-atom co-doped carbon materials can significantly improve the battery performance. Compared to single heteroatom doping, dual heteroatom doping contributes more in terms of interlayer distance, electronic conductivity and ion storage [58]. Thus, CDs have a deep potential for battery applications.

Primitive CDs are usually composed of carbon and oxygen functional groups. However, this monolithic composition can leave CDs lacking in application-specific properties, and the construction of functional groups in CDs is essential for them to fulfil specific functions. For example, amino groups enhance pseudo-capacitance. Hydroxyl and carboxyl groups help to improve the wettability between the electrolyte and the electrode (separator), thus enhancing the ion diffusion efficiency. In addition, with their abundant surface functional groups, CDs can be coordinated to a number of metal ions, and particle size and morphology tuning of the products can be achieved. In recent years, the functional groups constructed by CDs in batteries have induced the uniform deposition of ions in the batteries and effectively inhibited the growth of dendrites. Therefore, understanding the surface modification strategies of CDs can better enable the construction of CDs required for batteries.

Amide modification is the most widely used for modifying CDs. Since CDs surface is enriched with amino or carboxyl groups, they can be aminated with amino, acyl compounds. A treatment of organic precursors with oxidizing agents synthesizes CDs that are typically carboxyl-rich and can be covalently modified. As shown in Fig. 6 a, Chai et al. first prepared CQDs using activated carbon powder oxidized in mixed acids [67]. EDC·HCl and NHS were then added to the CQDs solution. The large number of carboxyl groups in the prepared CQDs allowed them to be covalently bonded to dopamine by the EDC/NHS coupling reaction. Amino-enriched CDs were prepared using urea, polyethylene glycol and ethylenediamine as co-reaction reagents. Therefore, amine, acyl compound condensation can also be used as a surface modification of amine-rich CDs by EDC-NHS-catalyzed reactions [68]. Of course, there are many other examples of introducing other groups on the surface by amide reaction, which are not listed here. More interestingly, coupling modifications between CDs and other quantum dots or nanoparticles can be realized by this reaction. As shown in Fig. 6b, Wang et al. chemically linked the quantum dots of carboxyl groups to amino-modified CDs via an EDC/NHS coupling reaction [69]. The amide reaction is now used as the most efficient and common means of surface modification of CDs, providing the basis for the functional construction of current CDs.

Undoubtedly, due to the abundance of surface groups on CDs, more chemical reactions can be introduced for modification purposes. For example, amino-containing CDs can be modified to introduce sulfur atoms on the surface by a sulfonylation reaction that attaches various sulfonyl chloride compounds to the surface. As shown in Fig. 6c, Tian et al. were able to obtain DC-modified CDs by acyl-sulfonation reaction by stirring nitrogen-doped CDs with dansulfonyl chloride (DC) in acetone solution at room temperature [70]. Since the surface functional groups usually contain reactive hydrogen, CDs have the ability to be modified by copolymerization reactions with cyclic or double-bonded compounds [71]. There are some other reaction methods to modify CDs, such as silane reaction, esterification reaction, etc., which are similar to the modification methods introduced before, and will not be expanded in detail here.

Compared to chemically reactive covalent modifications, non-covalent modification of CDs can provide new functional groups that target surface molecules. Based on CDs complexation, electrostatic or π interactions with the modified molecules. Complexation is achieved by ligand interactions between metal ions and CDs. As shown in Fig. 6d, Guo et al. heated CA powder in air to obtain carboxylic acid-passivated CDs [72]. Disperse the CDs in water and then adjust the pH with NaOH. As a result, the carboxylic acid groups in the CDs were neutralized, and Na+ went to the surface of the CDs by complexation to give Na+-functionalized CDs. Through the π-structure that CDs possess, they can be modified by interacting with small aromatic molecules that have a π-structure [73]. The most prominent advantage of noncovalent modifications is structural integrity, which can be characterized by constructing noncovalent modifications based on the surface features of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrophilic and hydrophobic modification of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nanosized CDs have the potential for better dispersion in media, and modulation of their hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity is an important aspect of CDs for battery applications. Several types of hydrophilic/hydrophobic modulation of CDs will be briefly outlined below.

The simplest method of regulation is to achieve hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity through precursors or solvents. As shown in Fig. 7 a, Mao et al. used the hydrophobic imidazole IL 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BmimPF6) as the sole carbon source in the H3PO4-ethanol medium [74]. Adjustment of the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of the produced CDs (or their ratios) can be achieved by simple control of the H3PO4/ethanol ratio. H3PO4/ethanol molar ratios in the range of 0–1.72 were obtained for both hydrophilic and hydrophobic CDs, whereas hydrophilic or hydrophobic CDs were obtained from H3PO4-BmimPF6 or BmimPF6 only, respectively. Undoubtedly, the time and temperature of the reaction will also have an effect on the hydrophilicity of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 7b, Dai et al. obtained CDs with different hydrophilicity by controlling different temperatures and reaction times with ethylenediamine and CA [75]. In contrast, the shorter the reaction time and the lower the reaction temperature, the better the hydrophilicity of the prepared CDs. Water decomposition and zinc dendrite growth in aqueous batteries can be well improved by utilizing hydrophobic electrolyte filling [76]. Therefore, the development of hydrophobic carbon materials is also extremely important for battery materials. The design of hydrophobic CDs lies mainly in the control, or modification, of their surface groups. Hydrophobic CDs (H-CDs) were prepared by solvothermal method using acetic acid/dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) solution and melamine by Yang et al. As shown in Fig. 7c, hydrophobic interactions led to the aggregation of H-CDs when water was introduced [16]. The enriched symmetric heterocyclic surface pyridine groups and epoxy groups realize the hydrophobicity of H-CDs.

Amphiphilic CDs benefit from their hydrophilic and lipophilic properties and can be used in a wider range of applications. Many research works have also realized the preparation of amphiphilic CDs. For example, amphiphilic CDs were obtained through the use of white berries as a carbon source by Omer et al. [77]. As shown in Fig. 7d, aqueous and organic solvents are well dispersed by CDs. In addition to obtaining amphiphilic CDs directly from biomass materials, amphiphilic CDs can also be obtained by the construction of hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups by coworkers during the synthesis of CDs. As shown in Fig. 7e, amphiphilic CDs were prepared by selecting 1,8-diaminooctane as the carbon source for the construction of hydrophobic groups and CA as the hydrophilic carbon source [78]. The prepared CDs contain hydrophilic amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups as well as hydrophobic alkyl chains, resulting in amphiphilicity. Recently, amphiphilic binders have shown strong advantages in battery materials [79]. Amphiphilic CDs are expected to be used as modified materials for binders.

The abundance of modulation strategies for CDs (from size control to atom doping, surface modification) is important for realizing effective design for applications. A large number of existing modulation methods have basically realized all the required size, atoms, and functional groups. These can be used chemically or physically as battery materials to provide high rate performance, high capacity, and superior cycling stability, depending on the needs of the different batteries. The specific details are described in more detail for the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of CDs in batteries and practical applications
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, on the basis of the research on the basic properties and modification means of CDs, it makes CDs show a high potential in the application of batteries. At present, the application of CDs in batteries is mainly through the following ways to achieve excellent performance. CDs can not only realize the regulation of its own morphology, but also through the coordination to regulate the morphology of other materials, stabilize the structure of the material, so as to obtain more active sites, enhance the storage of ions, strengthen the transport process, enable the battery to have a better performance of cycling stability. And it can be constructed by active groups or doped by heteroatoms, which can effectively inhibit dendrite growth, polysulfide shuttling, parasitic reactions, and can well regulate SEI or open up new application scenarios for battery materials. Therefore, this section will elucidate in detail the role of CDs in batteries and its actual performance in battery applications, thus providing new ideas and strategies for the design of CDs.

The CDs surface is enriched with functional groups, making it easy to achieve functional group customization. Some of these groups can be coordinated with metal ions, leading to changes in the morphology and particle size of the metal oxides, thus realizing the modulation of the morphology of the metal oxides [80,81]. Naturally, the introduction of CDs can also form new configurations for some carbon-based materials. In this subsection, several mechanisms of CDs in regulating morphology will be elucidated in several aspects.

First of all, the incorporation of CDs can promote or inhibit the growth of crystalline materials, thus realizing the modulation of material morphology. By introducing CDs, Jing et al. induced the formation of octahedral structure in the morphology of Mn3O4 particles in composites [82]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the formation mechanism of Mn3O4/CDs and Mn3O4 is given. The manganese atoms on the surface of the Mn electrode were first oxidized into a high-valent manganese oxide (MnOx) film at the anode, and the high-valent MnOx was reduced to stable Mn3O4 nanocrystals by electrochemical stripping. The difference is that CDs are added during the electrochemical stripping process. The CDs introduction can increase the system viscosity (η). Based on the Stokes-Einstein equation, D = constant/η, and an increase in η leads to slower diffusion of nanoparticles. Therefore, the size of pure Mn3O4 is much smaller than that in the composite. In addition, CDs can be trapped at Mn3O4 nanocrystals due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups (CO, CO), the self-assembly of Mn3O4 particles can be facilitated, which can well coat the CDs on the surface of Mn3O4, and the size of the Mn3O4/CDs composite can be larger than Mn3O4 nanoparticles without CDs. It is noteworthy that the octahedral Mn3O4 with (1 0 1) facets in the composites exhibits a superior electrochemical behavior than Mn3O4 nanoparticles, despite the larger size of the Mn3O4/CDs samples. Especially, the reversible discharge capacity of Mn3O4/CDs as an LIBs anode material is nearly five times that of pure Mn3O4. In contrast, crystal growth and stacking can also be effectively inhibited by the introduction of CDs, resulting in a unique structure. Jiang et al. introduced N-CDs into FeCO3, and the FeCO3/N-CDs composites yielded FeCO3 nanorods with smaller size and higher specific surface area compared with pristine FeCO3 [83]. The results show that N-CDs can inhibit the FeCO3 crystal growth and prevent the nanorods from being tightly stacked during the FeCO3 crystal growth process, which ultimately leads to the formation of multistage porous nanocomposites.

In addition, CDs can control the crystal growth process of metallic materials and thus affect the final morphology. CDs are suitable as nucleation sites because of the abundance of edge defects. Liu et al. modified Na3V2(PO4)2F3 (NVPF) with CQDs [84]. As shown in Fig. 8b, NVPF@CQDs are uniform and well-organized layered microspheres, whereas pure NVPF is a bare rectangle. The mechanism of formation of the new structure is elucidated in Fig. 8c. The abundant edge defects of CDs can serve as nucleation sites. In the primary stage, the raw material generates initial NVPF nuclei under solvothermal conditions, which serve as growth seeds. After that, secondary micro-rectangles start to crystallize in CQDs-induced defects with multiple surface functional groups. Lastly, the microsphere precursors were annealed to provide a better crystallinity of the product, accompanied by the organic carbon source decomposition to obtain the final structure. It was impressive that the NVPF@CQDs electrode had amazing rate capability at 20C and 30C. CQDs can be riveted to the surface of water-containing metal oxides, forming specialized structures based on “directional attachment” to a crystalline plane. CQDs-coupled TiO2(CTO) and pristine TiO2(TO) were prepared by Chen et al. [85]. The spherical structure of TO consists of a large number of nanosheets possessing smooth surfaces (Fig. 8d). The individual spherical structure of CTO is composed of many nanoscale rough branches, which exhibit a unique pinecone-like morphology (Fig. 8e). CQDs are rich in hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups, making them easy to anchor on the surface of hydrous TiO2 crystal nuclei. These CQDs can have a special influence on the process of attachment and assembly of tiny TiO2 nuclei. Since the ion-selective adsorption significantly affects the crystal surface growth and shape of nanocrystalline particles, this could be influenced by the CQDs enriched with functional groups on their surfaces. During seed grain formation, CQDs additives on the surface of TiO2 grains hinder their extension or aggregation and ultimately evolve into carbon layers on crystalline TiO2. Combining a fine layered structure with a unique surface composition provides synergistic benefits to achieve facilitated kinetics, increased electrolyte penetration area, improved electronic conductivity, and shorter Na+ diffusion lengths, resulting in significantly higher rate capacity and long-term recyclability.

Moreover, CDs function as “design additives”, and the construction of specific morphologies can be realized through self-assembly and cross-linking of CDs. As shown in Fig. 8f, Zhang et al. induced tiny TiO2 nascent nuclei upon addition of CDs, and these nuclei grew into one-dimensional linear TiO2 nanoneedles, which then merged with neighboring nanoneedles and self-assembled into a 3D petal-like structure due to the reduction of their surface energy [86]. Their petal-like structure consisting of nano-needles facilitates the shortening of the diffusion pathway of sodium ions. Hou et al. proposed a “CDs-induced ion adsorption-multipoint surface nucleation-crosslinking agglomeration in-situ growth” mechanism for metal oxides in-situ growth on CDs to form MxOy/CDs composites with porous pomegranate-like, which is shown in Fig. 9 a [87]. In ethanol, the addition of SnCl4-5H2O does not hydrolyze quickly to form Sn(OH)4. Thus, a uniformly dispersed solution of Sn4+ and CDs can be obtained after the introduction of CDs. First, due to the negative charges of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, Sn4+ can be absorbed into the surface of CDs by electrostatic interactions. Next, the absorbed Sn4+ can be converted into multiple SnO2 nuclei as the solvothermal reaction begins, and finally form CDs which have multiple SnO2 nuclei on the surface (called multi-SnO2@CDs). Finally, as the hydrothermal reaction is conducted, the agglomeration effect of CDs induces cross-linking and isotropic agglomeration growth between different multi-SnO2@CDs particles. This results in the formation of structures with a pomegranate-like shape. In summary, the formation mechanism of SnO2/CDs composites went through the three following stages: (1) Metal ion adsorption on the CDs surface, (2) Metal oxide multi-point nucleation on the CDs surface, and (3) Different multi-MxOy@CDs particles cross-linking agglomeration. The method is universal for metal oxides and has been successfully employed for the preparation of SnO2/CDs, Fe2O3/CDs and Cu2O/CDs composites, respectively. The engineered SnO2/CDs composites were used in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as anode materials, which effectively enhanced LIB's rate performance and cycle stability.

It is important to note that the dosage of CDs is very important when conditioning the material, which can have a great impact on the material morphology. As shown in Fig. 9b, with the increase of CDs dosage, the original striped fibrous polypyrrole can be induced into bead-like particles and accompanied by an increase of active particles [88]. Compared with pure polypyrrole-derived carbon, CDs-modulated polypyrrole-derived carbon exhibits superior electrochemical properties and better structural stability.

Volume expansion is an essential factor limiting battery cycling performance, which seriously affects the service life of batteries. Volume expansion remains a serious issue and is the focus of worldwide attention. According to their reaction mechanism, electrode materials undergo continuous self-crushing due to large volume changes. As a result, mechanical stress and fracture of the solid electrolyte interface are inevitable, which results in capacity decay and reduced adhesion life [89]. The introduction of CDs can effectively enhance the structural stability, inhibit the volume expansion, and mitigate its adverse effects. Currently, CDs are mainly used to enhance the structural stability of materials by inhibiting aggregation, chemical bonding, increasing surface area, and enhancing self-healing [90–92].

The utilization of CDs to inhibit aggregation and mitigate volume expansion in the charge/discharge process is the most common and effective strategy to increase the structural stability of materials. There have been many studies utilizing CDs to modify materials (such as VO2 [93,94], Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F [95], carbon nanotubes [15], VSe2 [96], etc.), in order to achieve ultra-stable structures for use in electrode materials and to enhance cycling stability. For example, Sn is an ideal material for the sodium-ion battery anode, limits the long-term stability of the batteries due to the volumetric expansion and the tendency to be crushed during cycling as a result of the unstable structure of the Sn-based electrodes. Liu et al. utilized nitrogen-doped GQDs with edge-anchored Sn nanodots as pillars in reduced graphene oxide blocks (NGQD/Sn-NG) to achieve a stable anode material structure. (Fig. 10 a) [97] GQDs can capture a large amount of Sn2+ rapidly owing to the strong electronegative carboxyl groups anchored along a conjugated sp2 carbon network edge, such as the “octopus” structure. Graphene oxide (GO) was then spontaneously assembled with it via electrostatic forces to form GQDs/Sn2+ complexed columnar GO block. After thermal reduction treatment under NH3 atmosphere, the Sn nanodots can be anchored along the edges of the GQDs through covalent SnOC/SnNC bonds, thus serving as the backbone for embedding the multilayered graphene blocks, thus ensuring the fast transfer of ions/electrons. Above all, the synergistic effect of spatial (layered graphene structure) and chemical (covalent heterogeneous interface) constraints not only effectively inhibits Sn aggregation in the heat treatment process, but also serves as a physical barrier to buffer the volume change of sodiation/ desodiation. Owing to the tight interfacial bonding of ultra-small Sn nanocrystals with carbon, robust layered structure and abundant channels for ion/electron transfer, the electrode exhibits superior rate performance and cycling stability. A high binding energy of COOH groups, pyridine N and pyrrole N with Sn was confirmed by density-functional theory (DFT) calculations, which can effectively prevent Sn nanocrystals from aggregating and decaying during the sodiation/desodiation process (Fig. 10b).

Besides achieving relief from volume expansion by inhibiting the aggregation of materials, it is also possible to stabilize the structure through the stabilizing effect of chemical bonding, or by physically providing a large surface, soft matrix or volume space [98–100]. Qing et al. utilized the chemical bonding effect constructed by CDs to effectively relieve the expansion stress of MoO2 [101]. As shown in Fig. 10c, MoO2-VC suffered severe structural damage after cycling. The cracked parts of the MoO2 particles in the electrically isolated regions limit further electrode reactions, which leads to a rapid loss of capacity. In contrast, in Fig. 10d, CDs were combined with MoO2 via MoOC, which could stabilize the particles of primary MoO2-CDs from the inside and connect the particles of MoO2-CDs externally during lithiation/delithiation. Such combinations of CDs and MoO2 can release the stress caused by volume expansion effectively. In addition to chemical effects, physical effects can be utilized to relieve the stress of volume expansion with CDs to provide a large specific surface area or volume. Chen et al. conveniently prepared a cotton-like protective layer formed on the surface of the NP-SiAl backbone with a Si5Al95 binary alloy selectively dealloyed and then surface-bonded with GQDs. (Fig. 10e) [102]. The prepared NP-SiAl/GQDs composites consisted of a bimodal SiAl network backbone and an interconnected GQDs protective layer distributed uniformly on the surface. The external GQDs in situ encapsulation on the nanoporous surface acts as a dual functional layer, providing superior electrical conductivity for Si, the porous nanoscale structure can provide enough space on the Si anode for volume expansion in charge/discharge process.

The dissolution and corrosion of electrode materials in the electrolyte will significantly affect the cycle stability and capacity retention of the battery [103]. The dissolution and corrosion of materials can be effectively inhibited by the modification of CDs. Guo et al. designed and investigated MnO2 nanowires anchored to GQDs as anode materials for ZIBs [104]. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping image of MnO2-GQDs is shown in Fig. 11 a, indicating a homogeneous distribution of Mn, O, and C elements along the nanowires, with the GQDs uniformly riveted to the MnO2. The GQDs riveted to the surface inhibit the dissolution of Mn during cycling. Therefore, the Coulombic efficiency of the MnO2-GQDs electrode is close to 100 % after 100 cycles, over four times that of pure MnO2. Constructing an electrostatic shielding layer at the anode is an effective means of avoiding corrosion [105]. Wang et al. employed positively charged chlorinated GQDs (Cl-GQDs) introduced in ZIBs to avoid corrosive effects by constructing a dual-function dynamic adaptive interface on the anode surface [106]. As shown in Fig. 11b, direct exposure of zinc anodes to active water with increased specific surface area also accelerates their corrosion, leading to degradation and destruction of the electrode structure. Contrastingly, the anodes showed distinct morphology evolution behaviors for electrolyte mediated by Cl-GQDs. During the charging process, the positively charged Cl-GQDs are rapidly and uniformly adsorbed on the surface of the negatively charged Zn anode, creating a dynamic electrostatic shielding layer that is used for dense galvanization. Furthermore, there is a relatively hydrophobic effect of the chlorinated groups, which slows down zinc anode corrosion by water molecules and prolongs its durable operation. (Fig. 11c). In addition, upon potential reversal, a dynamic reconfiguration behavior is exhibited as the Cl-GQDs are redispersed back to the electrolyte. Since there is no depletion of Cl-GQDs in the whole battery operation, dynamic adaptive interphase stability and continuity can be realized.

In recent years, self-healing materials have attracted widespread attention due to their recoverable morphology during charging and discharging. Hollow Ga2O3@nitrogen-doped CQDs (H-Ga2O3@N-CQDs) nanospheres with self-healing properties were prepared as an anode material for LIBs by Guo et al. [107]. Fig. 11d, e illustrates the structural changes during the (dis)charging process. Firstly, generated Ga droplets can release subsequent alloying reaction volume expansion during charging process. Secondly, the role of self-regulating structures to mitigate volume changes can be realized through hollow structures. The two points above are beneficial in maintaining the structural integrity of the material, but have proven to be insufficient. Finally, the volume expansion can be modulated by the N-CQDs coating while providing efficient pathways for Li+ and electron transport. Generated Ga during the conversion reaction can be confined to the coated shell, creating superior cycling stability. As a result, the nanosphere morphology can be preserved well. The self-healing ability of H-Ga2O3@N-CQDs and the material framework improve the battery cycling performance. Research on CDs for preparing self-healing materials is currently less and needs to be explored in depth by more researchers.

CDs have a quasi-spherical structure with channels that can more easily facilitate the transportation of ions [108]. The extreme reduction in the size of the quantum state leads to a quantum effect, where the maximized properties prescribed for effective ion diffusion, electron transfer, and charge transfer are realized. Meanwhile, the potential ability of quantum dots as battery electrode materials is demonstrated, not only as a single material to be used directly as the battery anode, but also for the modification of existing materials [82,109]. The mechanism by which CDs act in these transmissions will be discussed in detail in this section.

The most obvious feature of CDs is in the shortening of the ion diffusion distance [98]. Javed et al. investigated ion diffusion in LIBs and SIBs with the most basic CDs [110]. The large amount of Li+ embedded in the CDs during the initial cycling may prevent further Li+ transportation, thus affecting the capacity negatively. However, the expansion of the layer spacing starts to favor the de-embedding of Li+ from the CDs as the cycling continues. The quantum-sized particles provide a shorter length for ion diffusion as well as enhanced battery capacity by increasing the wettability and contact area between the CDs electrode and the electrolyte. In SIBs, a similar effect is observed. Quantum-sized particles help to reduce the Li+/Na+ diffusion distance and provide a continuous conduction pathway, resulting in high storage capacity of CDs.

Certainly, more research efforts still use CDs on the modification of electrode materials to change the transport process and enhance the electrical conductivity [92,111]. Yin et al. evaluated the cycling ability of NiO@Co3O4@GQDs and NiO@Co3O4 electrodes [100]. As shown in Fig. 12 a, due to the GQDs modification, the significant improvement in the cycling performance of NiO@Co3O4@GQDs compared to NiO@Co3O4. Li+ jump through adjacent COOH sites in the COOH layer of GQDs and act as reservoirs for lithium and electrolyte. As a result, it contributes to the electrochemically active interface enrichment and diffusion pathway shortening of Li+. As shown in Fig. 12b, EIS tests were performed. The measured resistance of NiO@Co3O4@GQDs during charge transfer is lower than that of NiO@Co3O4, indicating that the charge transfer process is easier at the front electrode. Assuming that the Li+ transport conforms to Fick's second law, derivation of the diffusion coefficient of Li+ (DLi+) is based on the diffusion equation. As shown in Fig. 12c, the DLi+ of NiO@Co3O4@GQDs is significantly higher than that of unmodified NiO@Co3O4 electrodes under different discharge states, indicating that the GQDs modification can promote the Li+ diffusion during the cycle process. The fundamental reason is that the hydrophilic carboxyl groups of GQDs make the surface of NiO@Co3O4@GQD composites highly polar with good affinity for polar ions, which promotes Li+ transportation. In addition to a series of transport-friendly surface groups at CDs, CDs can further be used to optimize the transport process by controlling changes to the material structure [18]. As shown in Fig. 12d, Tian et al. utilized NVP (p-type) and CQDs (n-type) to design unique p-n heterojunction structures. The energy band structures of K0.1Na2.95V1.95Co0.05(PO4)3 (KC0.05) and CQDs were deduced based on the bad gap (Eg) and (EF − Ev) [112]. The valence band of the NVP is denoted Ev and the Fermi energy level is denoted EF. The KC0.05 Eg value is 3.44 eV, while the corresponding EF flattens out at 0.82 eV above the Ev, below the band gap midline. The majority of carriers are known to be holes for KC0.05 according to the Fermi-Dirac distribution function. All this justifies KC0.05 for a p-type conductor. Similarly, CQDs has relatively more carriers based on 4.04 eV of Eg and 2.07 eV of (EF − Ev) electrons, demonstrating that it is an n-type conductor. Naturally, p-n type interfacial heterojunctions are included in the KC0.05@CQDs composite. The energy band bending at the interface of the two phases is the same for the KC0.05 and CQDs. Notably, the electron mobility can be further improved by this heterojunction due to the fact that the hole carriers can drop down to relatively low energy levels across the interface, and thus the holes can be freely transported without being subjected to scattering and collisions by the Co2+. Therefore, due to their high electron mobility, electrochemical performance can be greatly improved, especially in rate capacity.

Taking it a step further, the latest study utilized theoretical calculations to reveal the effects of CDs during transport. Li et al. optimized the ion transport paths, lowered the ion diffusion barriers, and further increased the density of states of the material with highly electron-conducting CQDs to effectively improve the electrical conductivity of Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) [113]. The role of CQDs was further investigated using DFT calculations. In order to simulate the composition in CQDs modified LTO (M-LTO), C41 was placed on top of the (1 1 1) plane and Li-Ti terminated Li11Ti13O32 superlattice. This termination was chosen due to it has the lowest surface energy. For Li11Ti13O32 and Li11Ti13O32-C41, and the optimal Li-ion migration paths are illustrated in Fig. 12e. It is clearly observed that Li-ion migration paths in Li11Ti13O32 mainly undulate in a semicircular arc along the z-direction and look nearly straight along the z-direction. After adding carbon, the undulation is smaller in the z-direction. It mainly exhibits a semicircular arc along the XOY plane. Li11Ti13O32-C41 has a small energy barrier from the transition state to the reactants and products, implying that CQDs can improve the diffusion kinetics for lithium ions effectively. Moreover, density of states (DOS) calculations show that carbon loading can significantly increase electronic conductivity. As shown in Fig. 12f, the addition of C41 increases the electron density near the Fermi energy level and fills the energy gap in Li11Ti13O32. The partial density of states (PDOS) plot of C41 indicates that this gap is filled, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs can improve the electronic conductivity of LTO effectively. A series of combined experimental and theoretical calculations demonstrated the optimized transport process of CDs, which facilitates the conductivity of the material. It is noteworthy that CDs enhances the electronic conductivity of the material and can be used as a conductive agent to improve the rate capability of the battery [114,115].

Considering one of the most competitive systems for next-generation batteries, solid composite electrolyte lithium batteries are still limited by the slow diffusion of ions. The introduction of CDs can improve the transport performance of solid-state electrolytes significantly. CDs-based composite electrolytes achieve high ionic conductivity. First, and most commonly, CDs promote the dissociation of lithium salts, thereby providing additional ionic pathways [116]. Hou et al. used GQDs to enhance poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO)-based electrolytes [117]. As shown in Fig. 13 a, which demonstrates the Li+ transport path, the GQDs generated more free lithium ions by disrupting the PEO matrix crystallinity, creating more amorphous regions for ionic conduction, and facilitating the dissociation of lithium salts. Thus, composite polymer electrolytes (CPE) realized highly ionic conductivity and high transfer number. It is well known that Lewis base sites enrichment on the filler surface allows strong interaction with salt ions and enhancement of ionic conductivity. In this case, more Lewis base sites such as hydroxyl and amino groups may interact with the Li+ and influence the Li+ movement in the electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 13b, it was shown by DFT simulations that Li+ was trapped in pure PEO by the PEO chains, leading to a maximum free energy, in which state Li+ is difficult to move. When used in combination with NH2 and OH, the free energy is almost halved compared to pure PEO. In addition, Li+ NH2 is stronger than Li+ OH because the N atom has a stronger negative charge than the O atom. From the free energy calculations, it can be inferred that Li+ will have less resistance to shuttle between NH2 and OH, which is favorable for charge transfer. The CDs can hinder the migration of anions in the polymer, thus enhancing the ionic conductivity [118]. Li et al. prepared a new Li+ conductor based on CQDs by pyrolyzing poly (lithium 4-styrene sulfonate) and CA [119]. As shown in Fig. 13c, the staggered ethylene oxide (EO) chain segments suppressed the CQDs anion movement, but it was possible for smaller Li+ to be transported through the EO chain segments. The interaction between Li+ and the CQDs anion is weak, which means that there is no strong interference with Li+ movement by the CQDs. Therefore, an increase in tLi+ can be achieved without a decrease in δLi+. In addition, as shown in Fig. 13d, strong hydrogen bonds and a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups are formed between the CQDs and the PEO. The hydrogen-bonding interactions between CQDs and PEO and the strong interactions among other oxygen-containing functional groups also hinder anion migration. CQDs anions immobilized on PEO chain segments are hardly free to move and therefore Li+ transport number increases. In addition, CDs can inhibit the ion–solvent cluster formation, leading to the disruption of ion–solvent clusters and creating a space-charge layer, which promotes the transport of lithium ions [120].

Enhancing the ion storage capacity of materials has been a goal that many battery researchers have been striving for. In particular, compared with K+, Na+, Zn2+ and other batteries with larger radius of atomic size have more urgent need to develop materials with high ion storage capacity, so as to enhance the capacity of the battery. The rich tunable nature of CDs creates great potential for enhancing ion storage [121–124]. Enhancement of ion storage can be easily realized by providing electrode materials with larger specific surface area, defects, active sites, or by constructing functional groups or atoms that are beneficial for ion adsorption.

There are two main types of ways in which CDs can physically enhance ion storage, one through the structural properties of the CDs themselves (e.g., defects, specific surface area, etc.), and the other by modulating the structure of other materials. Due to the easy doping nature of CDs, this creates great possibilities for their modification. As shown in Fig. 14 a, Saroja et al. prepared GQDs, N-GQDs, B-GQDs, and investigated their storage properties for lithium and sodium ions [125]. After detailed studies, the presence of edge defects in (doped) GQDs has been shown to contribute to the enhancement of lithium and sodium ion electrochemical storage. More active sites are available to store ions due to the high specific surface area. The sources of specific surface area provided by CDs are divided into two, one is the use of CDs to compound on the material surface to offer additional surface area, and the other is the modulation of the material's original structure to offer additional surface area. For example, Zou et al. doped CQDs in lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO), and the CQDs provided a large number of Li+ storage sites owing to its abundant pore space and large specific surface area, which provided excellent specific capacity for the corresponding electrodes [126]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 14b, Wu et al. effectively tuned the morphology of TiO2 by using CQDs to present a hierarchical nanostructure organized by many thin nanosheets with interconnected pores [127]. The obtained material has a large surface area, thus significantly improving the storage capacity of sodium ions. CDs can modulate the position of the p-orbital energy level, thereby enhancing ion storage activity. Huang et al. utilized CDs and porous N-doped carbon fibers to succeed in enhancing NiO nanosheets (CF/ECF/NiO/CD) for lithium-ion storage by modulating the position of the p-orbital energy levels [128]. The Li ion adsorption behavior on CF/NiO, CF/ECF/NiO/CD, CF/ECF/NiO hybrids was investigated using DFT calculations. As shown in Fig. 14c, both CF/ECF/NiO/CD and CF/ECF/NiO exhibited electron accumulation on the oxygen surface around ECF, suggesting a more powerful capacity for charge transfer. Upon introduction of CDs, slices of the CDs surface in CF/ECF/NiO/CD showed more charge accumulation around Ni, suggesting that the enhanced binding energy between the hybridized surface and adsorbates can perform the most powerful charge transfer and Li+ adsorption. During Li+ transportation, the optimal Gibbs free energy change (ΔGLi*) required for the discharge process of lithium ions was determined. ΔGLi* (Ea, eV) value is closer to zero (0 eV), lithium ions and kinetics are transported faster. According to Fig. 14d, the two peaks represent the NiO region (ts-1/3/5) and C region (ts-2/4/6) of ΔGLi*, respectively. The CF/ECF/NiO/CD with Li+ embedded in the NiO region has the smallest ΔGLi*, indicating that the capping of CDs on NiO reduces the adsorption energy, which enhances Li+ for fast transmission and higher capacity. The ΔGLi* of Li+ embedded in C region has a similar pattern. In addition, the ΔGLi* values of Li+ embedded in C are higher than those of NiO, indicating that the contribution of the C region to the capacity is lower than that of the NiO region. The Li+ transport close to the Fermi energy level (Ef) is further analyzed in terms of the density of states (DOS) mainly from the adsorbed energy states in the p-band. As shown in Fig. 14e, CDs were introduced to move the CF/ECF/NiO/CD p-band center away from Ef, indicating that the bonded states are strongly adhesive and enhance the internal electronic interactions. Fig. 14f shows that CDs and CF/ECF are farther away from Ef than CF. Meanwhile CDs and CF/ECF designs have the farthest center of the p-orbital with respect to Ef. Therefore, the CF/ECF/NiO/CD outstanding lithium storage performance is derived from the p-orbital displacement, which makes NiO a high-capacity for LIBs anode.

Except for the physical strategy to enhance ion storage, the storage of ions can also be realized by chemical strategy. In the previous section, it was mentioned that CDs are easy to regulate the surface groups, so the ion trapping can be enhanced by the surface groups, thus enhancing the storage capacity. Oxygen-containing functional groups (OFGs) of CDs are particularly important for the dissociative adsorption of ions [129]. As shown in Fig. 15 a, Zhao et al. prepared CDs/rGO composites with CDs and reduced graphene oxide [130]. CDs can be used to modulate the surface functional groups of composites by introducing CO bonds through depletion of CO bonds. As shown in Fig. 15b, the introduction of CDs leads to an increase in the amount of CO groups and is accompanied by a decreasing trend in the number of CO groups. The hydroxyl group can act as a nucleophilic reagent in CDs by epoxidation reactions attacking the epoxy on rGO through ring-opening, leading to CO reduction and depletion. Moreover, when the CDs/rGO weight ratio reaches 0.6, the CO group content increases. This spatial site blockage, possibly due to severe CDs clustering, prevents the epoxy group on rGO from being attacked by the hydroxyl group on the CDs. It is well known that the redox reaction mechanism between Na+ and CO groups (CO + Na+ + e− ↔ CONa). As a result, a higher fraction of CO groups are present in CDs/rGO compared to other samples, thus providing more sites for reversible reactions with Na+, thus enhancing the capacity. On the other hand, the introduction of different heteroatoms can have a significant effect on the storage of ions. A method for large-scale preparation of CDs was developed by Li et al. [19]. Six N-doped and O-containing material models were constructed for pyrrole nitrogen, graphite nitrogen, pyridine nitrogen, carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Pyrrole N (N-5), pyridine N (N-6), and graphite N (N-Q) correspond to N atoms inside a pentagonal pyrrole ring, a hexatomic ring containing N atoms, and N atoms bonded to three carbon atoms, respectively. In the adsorption energy calculations, an optimized geometry of hollow pores with unsaturated carbon at the edges was obtained by adding a K atom to these negative sites. Calculations confirm that pyridine N- and O-doping provide enhanced potassium storage capacity, as N-6 doping generates more defects and increases the number of available active sites for potassium storage, whereas containing O offers enriched active sites for energy storage.

There is no doubt that combining the two strategies to design materials can yield maximum benefits. While obtaining an excellent structure, atoms and groups are introduced to enhance ion storage [15,131]. As shown in Fig. 15d, Liu et al. proposed a new strategy for the synthesis of edge nitrogen-rich CDs columnar graphene blocks (N-CDGBs) by aniline self-polymerization into graphene oxide blocks and subsequent carbonization [132]. Dense N-CDGBs exhibit strong structural stability and more active sites for sodium storage due to high stacking density and integrated lamellar structure with large edge layer spacing and high edge nitrogen content.

Dendrites growth can negatively impact the electrochemical performances of batteries. When dendrites appear inside the battery, they can cause the electrolyte liquid or solid electrolyte film inside the battery to be mechanically damaged, increasing the internal resistance of the battery, decreasing electrochemical performance and cycle life. In addition, dendrites may also cause inside the battery thermal runaway or short circuit and other safety hazards, affecting the battery's stability and safety. The introduction of CDs can effectively alleviate the dendrite problem of the battery by regulating the ion nucleation process, or inducing crystal surface growth.

Primarily, the introduction of CDs provides uniform nucleation sites, thus avoiding the formation of dendrites [133–135]. Wang et al. effectively alleviated the interfacial concentration gradient by adding N, S co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) to the electrolyte, thereby promoting uniform nucleation and deposition of Zn [136]. Since the NS-CQDs contain enriched oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces, they have the ability to react with the zinc foil to generate a uniformly charged NS-CQDs layer. As shown in Fig. 16 a, the NS-CQDs layer can offer plentiful nucleation sites for the pre-seeding and nucleation of Zn2+. Due to negligibly charged CDs close to the Zn surface, the ion concentration gradient can be effectively reduced. The homogenization of the electric field at the Zn-electrolyte interface facilitated the rapid transfer of Zn2+, resulting in dendrite-free Zn deposition.

Meanwhile, the introduction of CDs can also reduce the nucleation barrier by lowering the nucleation potential [137,138]. This can generate more nucleation sites, so that the metal ions preferentially bind to the CDs, achieving uniform deposition of zinc ions and avoiding dendrite growth. Zhang et al. used GQDs with enriched oxygen-containing groups as additives for aqueous Zn ion batteries [139]. The interaction between oxygenated GQDs and Zn2+ was investigated by DFT. As shown in Fig. 16b, model structures were designed to place Zn2+ in each of the three functional groups (carboxyl, ether, hydroxyl groups). The results show that Zn2+ has stronger adsorption on carboxyl, ether, hydroxyl groups compared to pure Zn. This is mainly attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, since the electron-deficient Lewis acidic Zn2+ can interact strongly with electron-rich Lewis bases. Thus, it displayed stronger adsorption of Zn2+, which allowed the GQDs to be used as a medium to direct uniform Zn deposition in the circulation process. As shown in Fig. 16c, in an electrolyte without GQDs, zinc deposits on the surface are generated due to random deposition of zinc ions on the solid–liquid interface resulting in uneven zinc foils. These Zn deposits act as charge-rich centers in the electric field and further exacerbate the Zn dendrite growth by continuous deposition on the tip. When GQDs are present in the electrolyte, the GQDs are predominantly adsorbed on the surface of Zn and dissolved in the electrolyte, which can regulate the zinc ions through stronger binding energy oxygen-containing groups, thus inducing homogeneous zinc deposition. The increase in nucleation sites is due to the lower barrier for nucleation, which effectively inhibits the zinc dendrite growth. Similarly, this mechanism of action can also act in LIBs [140]. Li et al. improved the performance of LIBs by using N, S-CDs as a modulating additive [141]. As shown in Fig. 16d, similar to that in zinc-ion batteries, the random deposition of lithium ions during cycling leads to lithium dendrite formation. On the contrary, the N, S-CDs possessing many lithophilic sites can preferentially adsorb Li ions on the surface, and then be further uniformly deposited on the substrate. As a result, a dense and smooth Li deposition layer is realized in N, S-CDs, leading to the batteries' prolonged cycling stability and high capacity retention.

Interestingly, the addition of CDs induces crystal surface orientation and radically avoids the Zn dendrite formation [142]. As shown in Fig. 16e, The Zn2+ can be adsorbed onto the zincophilic N, S-CDs, creating a positively charged surface [143]. The positively charged N, S-CDs are then transported to the anode (co-deposition) driven by electric field forces, leading to homogeneous nucleation of Zn deposits. At the same time, the lattice match between the Zn (0 0 2) plane and the N, S-CDs graphene structure is excellent, and the strong adsorption between Zn2+ and N, S-CDs facilitates the priority Zn microplate growth along the (0 0 2) plane, which provides a large number of Zn deposits with (0 0 2) basal planes on the surface of the Zn anode. The deposits have the additional advantage of uniform interfacial charge density and high Zn2+ trapping capacity.
