Subsequently, zinc deposits can be induced to grow in the same selective direction along the (0 0 2) plane due to the presence of these special co-deposits, resulting in a zinc anode without dendrites. The growth direction of the microplates can be adjusted in a controlled manner throughout the growth process, which allows the method to more easily to enhance the constitutive design of the battery material.

Lithium-sulfur batteries have great potential to replace conventional LIBs due to their high energy density and theoretical cathode capacity. However, the notorious lithium polysulfides (LiPSs) causes rapid capacity degradation and low Coulombic efficiency when it dissolves and spreads within the electrolyte during the discharge/charge. CDs can chemically adsorb polysulfides due to the abundance of polar groups that can be constructed on their surfaces [144,145]. Therefore, lithium polysulfide shuttling is inhibited, which improves lithium-sulfur battery electrochemical environment.

Most commonly, CDs are used to modify cathodes or separators to achieve adsorption of lithium polysulfide. As shown in Fig. 17 a, Hu et al. prepared cathode materials using polyethyleneimine functionalized CDs (PEI-CDs) [146]. Sulfur was reduced to soluble polysulfides in the discharge process, and the amine groups on the PEI-CDs surface provided rich absorption sites to adsorb these polysulfides, which were confined within and/or around the invariant cathode structure. Thus, the dissolution of polysulfides is effectively inhibited. At the end of the discharge process, soluble LiPSs were converted to insoluble Li2S by further reduction on the PEI-CDs-modified surface. In addition to nitrogen-containing functional groups, some polar oxygen-containing functional groups can also achieve the same effect [147]. The chemical uptake of polysulfides can be significantly enhanced and the “shuttle effect” suppressed due to polar functional groups in GQDs that offer more active absorption sites. Further theoretical calculations show that in addition to nitrogen-containing groups, oxygen-containing groups can also have strong adsorption properties. The GQDs with different functional groups (OH, CO, COOH, and pyrrole-N) all have larger binding energy values, as shown in Fig. 17b, suggesting the existence of a strong chemical effect between polar groups and Li2S, especially the COOH functional group. Fig. 17c, d elucidates the electron density distributions between Li2S and GQDs, Li2S and GQDs with COOH, respectively. The electron densities of Li2S show partly overlapping with GQDs, whereas there is a significant overlap of the electron densities of Li2S and GQDs with COOH, revealing that COOH has a stronger chemical interaction with polysulfides. Similarly, the same effect can be obtained by using it as a separator modification [148]. A multi-walled carbon nanotube/ NCQDs (MWCNT/NCQDs) coated separator was designed for Li-S batteries as shown in Fig. 17e [149]. Oxygen-containing functional groups and N atoms on NCQDs can chemically adsorb polysulfides, thus enhancing the battery electrochemical performance.

In contrast, the direct use of CDs as additives simplifies the process of preparing battery materials even more. Inspired by the reversible process of thrombus formation and thrombolysis in blood vessels, Fu et al. developed a unique strategy for LiPSs encapsulation (Fig. 17f) [150]. A cleverly designed electrolyte additive (similar to platelets) can only interact with LiPSs, resulting in the formation of a recoverable protective layer in situ. This allows the protective layer to subtly form at the electrode–electrolyte interface, instead of closely encircling each sulfur particle as most have done previously. Moreover, since the additive maintains its dissolved state when sulfur is present at the electrode, the protective layer formed in situ will not sense the structural damage due to sulfur electrode volume changes during the S-Li2S transition. LiPSs additives should fulfill three criteria at least: well dispersion in the electrolyte, high interactivity with LiPSs, and favorable inhibition of the LiPSs subsequent dissolution by the recyclable protective layer.

In addition to the previously mentioned as an adsorption or protective layer to avoid the “shuttle effect”. However, this does not solve the fundamental problem of the presence of these polysulfides, and it is still difficult to avoid the shuttling of polysulfides. For this reason, the use of CDs to assist in the catalytic transformation of polysulfides is also a more efficient way to solve the problem of polysulfides. For example, Zhao et al. achieved a synergistic catalytic effect by assembling graphitic C3N4 (g-C3N4) with highly dispersed nitrogen-containing CQDs [151]. The introduction of the CQDs improved the conductivity of the g-C3N4 substrate. The catalyst can accelerate the kinetics of LiPSs conversion reaction, which accelerates the decomposition of Li2S during charging and the precipitation of Li2S during discharge. Efficient adsorption and electrochemical conversion performance were realized for LiPSs simultaneously. Thus, the shuttle effect is avoided and superior battery performance is achieved. Similarly, Song et al. also utilized the property of CQDs to enhance the electrical conductivity, thus accelerating the conversion process of polysulfides [152]. The non-conducting surface of VO2 restricts the direct nucleation and growth process of Li2S. Homogeneous loading of CQDs on its surface can effectively enhance the local electronic/ionic conductivity, which is favorable for the direct nucleation reaction of Li2S. By improving the surface structure and properties of the catalysts, the high sulfur release activity was achieved, which facilitates the design of high-performance soft-packed Li-S soft-packed batteries.

The SEI structure has a significant impact on the recyclability aspects of the metal anode. The ideal SEI layer has the following properties: high ionic conductivity, good mechanical strength, thin and stable thickness, resistance to volume changes, stable and wide operating temperature and operating voltage. SEI stabilization is an important indicator of battery safety performance.

The introduction of CDs promotes the formation of thin and uniform SEI [153,154]. Qian et al. utilized Li-absorbed and F-doped CDs to form LiF-modified carbonaceous layers after a self-assembly and carbonization process, which were coated on SiO particles to obtain SiO@LCD [155]. As shown in Fig. 18 a, LiF-carbon co-coating induces the decomposition of the electrolyte and generates a dense, thin, and homogeneous LiF-rich SEI film. This suppresses electrode volume expansion in the cycling process, which collectively contributes to the SiO@LCD electrode exhibiting significantly enhanced cycle stability and Coulombic efficiency. The introduction of CDs is extremely important for the stabilization of the SEI to homogenize the stresses during expansion and avoid SEI rupture. Ponraj et al. coated GQDs on 3D foam copper to relieve stress [149]. As shown in Fig. 18b, Li deposits form between the copper wire and the composite SEI layer. Since the lithium deposits are formed underneath the SEI layer, an outward stress on the SEI layer is exerted by the volumetric expansion created by the lithium plating. This stress causes the SEI layer to fracture at a certain capacity limit, accelerating formation of dendrites and driving the battery to failure. GQDs allow the Li plating to be uniformly distributed, thereby applying a uniform stress across the composite SEI layer. The absence of GQDs results in an irregular lithium plating layer due to uneven stress on the SEI layer and subsequent layer rupture. Due to their weaker inter-particle interactions, they can be compensated for the induced stresses by the re-arrangement of the GQDs in the composite SEI layer. In addition to stress relief to protect the SEI layer, CDs can also act as a protective layer avoiding direct contact with the electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 18c, when LTO's surface comes in touch with the electrolyte, the surface reacts, leading to a phase transition on the outermost surface of LTO [157]. However, when a protective layer of GQDs is present, the phase of the LTO's outermost surface is not changed or changes slightly. The gas produced by the phase transition on the LTO surface when GQDs are present can be maintained at a very low level. Therefore, surface modification with GQDs inhibits the LTO/electrolyte interfacial reaction and the resulting SEI film is thinner and smoother than the uncoated LTO electrode. The GQDs play a role in protecting the SEI thereby enhancing the Coulombic efficiency.

In addition to keeping the uniformity and stability of the SEI film from being destroyed, it is also necessary to ensure that the SEI film does not thicken, as an excessively thick film will increase the impedance of the battery, which is detrimental to the operation of the battery. For example, Lu et al. effectively suppressed the thickening of the SEI film using CDs [154]. The impedance of the CDs@rGO electrode did not increase significantly after 50 cycles, indicating the formation of a stable SEI film on the surface of the CDs@rGO electrode. In the rGO electrode, the charge transfer resistance kept increasing in 50 cycles, indicating that the SEI film continued to grow at the rGO electrode/electrolyte interface. This ensures the excellent rate performance and cycling performance of the CDs@rGO electrode. Zhang et al. avoided the direct contact between the FeF3-0.33H2O nanosheet array and the electrolyte by constructing a layer of GQDs on the surface of FeF3-0.33H2O nanosheets [153]. Only a small amount of SEI film will be formed on the surface of GQDs. The thickening of the SEI film in the subsequent reaction process is avoided. As a result, the overall conductivity of the electrode remains well and the capacity decay is slow, even after a long cycling period. On the contrary, the lack of protection in the FFNA electrodes results in the formation of a thick SEI film after a long cycling period, and FeF3-0.33H2O changes to FeF3-x-0.33H2O The electron transfer during the reaction process is limited by the thick SEI film, leading to an increase in the internal resistance of the cell and a drastic decrease in the capacity.

The introduction of CDs eliminates several major types of problems mentioned in previous sections and also solves some other problems in batteries. For example, CDs can be effective in avoiding some parasitic reactions. The most common way is to modulate the interaction with water molecules, thus avoiding some parasitic reactions [142]. As shown in Fig. 19 a, the parasitic reaction often occurs in the benchmark ZnSO4 electrolyte (BE), and Zhang et al. constructed an effective “H2O-sturcure-reguling” strategy to inhibit the H2O-induced parasitic reaction [158]. This strategy was realized by dispersing Zn-doped CDs (ZCDs) in a conventional BE to form an inorganic nanoparticle suspension electrolyte. The surface parasitic reaction of Zn anode is mainly caused by active solvated H2O. The well-designed ZCDs can self-enrich dynamically on the surface of the Zn anode and act as a modulation site to reduce the ratio of isolated H2O to solvated H2O. Thus, this special electrolyte markedly inhibited interface parasitic reactions induced by H2O. Undoubtedly, except for heteroatom doping, the introduction of some groups can also inhibit the parasitic reaction by interacting with water. Oxygen-containing groups of CDs stimulate interfacial hydrogen bonding [139], or surface-abundant polar groups can modulate the solvated structure of Zn2+ by decreasing the ligand-active H2O molecules [105], thus inhibiting the parasitic reaction. Moreover, CDs can modulate ions other than H2O that are prone to parasitic reactions. Zhang et al. introduced a quantum-sized functional layer of CDs at the Zn anode as shown in Fig. 19b [90]. The insoluble CDs layer can be used as a physical protective layer to avoid direct contact between the Zn electrode and water molecules. The electronegative functional groups of CDs can provide electrostatic interactions to exclude the incoming SO4 2-. As a result, the elimination of water-mediated parasitic reactions improved the Zn2+ reaction kinetics.

More importantly, the introduction of CDs can extend the application scenarios of battery materials. As shown in Fig. 19c, by introducing CDs with fluorescence as a bifunctional electrolyte additive into the electrolyte, the battery can achieve both multicolor emission and energy storage [21]. Interestingly, zinc ion batteries in the shape of fluorescent fibers with different fluorescent properties can be woven into textiles with multi-colored battery displays. It can therefore be used for rescue in dark environments and wearable energy supply. Thereby unlocking new application scenarios for batteries.

CDs have shown great potential in solving a variety of problems in batteries, solving several common thorny problems present in most batteries. This is mainly attributed to their richly tunable nature, which enables efficient configuration design. However, a more in-depth explanation of their detailed electrochemical mechanism of action has yet to be developed, which requires further research. And the most unique fluorescence property of CDs has not yet been well utilized, with only one recent paper developing new application scenarios. This requires more novel work to provide new ideas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical performance of CDs in battery applications
ce:section.ce:para: Benefit from a large amount of summarized work on the preparation and design of CDs [159–161]. Provides certain ideas for their use in battery material design. The practical application of CDs in batteries will be discussed in this section to provide insights and help for the later research work.

The first and most important thing that should be noted is the performance in Lithium secondary batteries that are necessary for daily life [162,163]. As shown in Fig. 20 a, the modification of NiO nanosheets using CDs successfully enhanced the lithium ion storage of NiO nanosheets [128]. This leads to a significant enhancement in the area capacity of the LIBs. In addition, NiO and carbon fibers themselves have excellent cycling stability. It is noteworthy that the modification of CDs retains their stability characteristics while enhancing the disadvantage of low area capacity. Secondly, sodium-ion batteries, which are an alternative to lithium, have also become more popular in recent research efforts [164–166]. As shown in Fig. 20b, the sodium-ion half-cell can even run stably at 200C for 20,000 cycles after being modified by CDs, which is closely related to the acceleration of ion migration and diffusion by CDs [167]. Very high energy densities can be provided in full batteries, with a value of 368.7 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 314 W kg−1 (Fig. 20c). However, the cycle life of the full battery is significantly lower than that of the half battery (Fig. 20d).

In order to improve the safety performance of batteries, aqueous and solid-state batteries have become the next new trend [168–170]. As shown in Fig. 20e, the Zn//MnO2 full battery without CQDs failed quickly, while the battery with CQDs additives could run stably for 500 cycles, showing better cycling stability [171]. The CQDs additives can indeed improve the cycling performance of the aqueous full batteries, which further proves their practicality. As shown in Fig. 20f, the use of CDs as an inorganic filler can alleviate part of the oxidation of PEO, exhibit higher cycling stability than PEO during charging and discharging, and have a stable coulombic efficiency that does not decay significantly, ultimately achieving a capacity retention rate of 72 % [172].

Although CDs have shown some potential for application in battery applications, performing better in mature systems such as lithium batteries. Full batteries do not perform as well in sodium electricity as half batteries maintain, and there are still greater difficulties in terms of practical applications for solid state batteries. And it's not hard to find that many of them use CDs to modify other substances to do something. But very little work has been done to investigate the direct use of CDs to replace the carbon materials needed in batteries (conductive agent, anode materials, etc.).

CDs show extraordinary potential in solving several thorny problems commonly found in battery materials. This review systematically summarized the recent advances of CDs, encompassing synthesis methods, mainstream modification strategies, and their applications in batteries. Most importantly, we focus on integrating the effects of CDs in batteries from a new perspective, distinguishing them from the traditional categorization of CDs in different batteries. A large number of preparation modes offered by the two main synthetic strategies provide the basis for the application of CDs. Modification of CDs offers more possibilities for the construction of their electrochemical properties, creating an efficient means for tailoring specific electrochemically favorable structures and compositions. The introduction of CDs can regulate the morphology change of the material and stabilize the structure of the material, thus obtaining more active sites, enhancing the ion storage, strengthening the transport process, and enabling the battery to have better cycle stability performance. Through effective active group construction or heteroatom doping, it can effectively inhibit dendrite growth, polysulfide shuttling, parasitic reactions, and can well regulate SEI, as well as unlock new application scenarios for battery materials.

As mentioned above, significant progress has been made in recent years in the research of CDs for battery applications, but there are still many problems in realizing their practical applications, and the following challenges need to be overcome for the future development of CDs for battery materials:
(1)
The primary problem facing the practical application of CDs is the large-scale preparation of CDs. Although some researches have reported that the large-scale synthesis of CDs can be realized by hydroxyaldol condensation reaction, etc., they do not indicate the purity of the obtained CDs, and the research work on the purity test of large-scale prepared CDs is needed. There is an urgent need to design superior separation and purification techniques to match the purification problems in large-scale production to ensure the purity of CDs, exclude the influence of impurities, and avoid the influence of unreacted precursors and solvents on the battery materials, which can lead to unstable battery performance. These purity issues may require more joint coordination between researchers related to chemical purification and equipment development to accomplish.
(2)
Despite the many satisfactory results of CDs in battery applications, the deeper electrochemical mechanisms of CDs in batteries have not yet been revealed. For example, how ions diffuse in CDs and how the process of shorter diffusion paths accelerating the diffusion rate takes place. These mechanisms are crucial for our deeper understanding of structure-effect relationships and modulation of electrochemical activity. In addition to some theoretical calculations and simulation processes, more advanced in-situ characterization techniques (in-situ Raman spectroscopy, in-situ Fourier transform infrared, in-situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy, etc.) can be considered for clarifying their electrochemical mechanisms.
(3)
Most of the research work on CDs still focuses on the modification of other materials (graphene, carbon nanotubes, MnO2, etc.), while less research has been conducted on the direct application of CDs. The advantages of CDs themselves are difficult to exploit, as they provide shorter ion transfer distances and shorten transfer paths due to their smaller size, larger specific surface area, and high number of defect sites. It is possible to consider whether CDs can be involved in both positive and negative electrodes as well as the separator, and whether the preparation of “all-CDs batteries” can realize high specific capacity and high rate at the same time. The direct use of CDs as battery materials may have great potential, which requires more efforts from researchers.
(4)
CDs have unique fluorescent properties that distinguish them from other carbon materials, but this property has not been well utilized in battery materials. Only in the aforementioned recent use in fiber batteries to achieve the dual purpose of energy storage and rescue. However, it has been reported that fluorescent materials are utilized to detect SEI in batteries. The research on using CDs as fluorescent probes has a deeper foundation, and some cross-techniques can be tried to exploit CDs for SEI detection. It is even possible to use CDs to accelerate the “in situ visualization” by reacting with the active material of the battery to realize more in situ labeling process through fluorescence, and to help reveal the electrochemical mechanism by using CDs. This will be a very practical and challenging work.
(5)
Combining machine learning with CDs-based materials holds great promise for optimal synthesis, property prediction. There has been a lot of research to control the synthesis of CDs as well as the prediction of properties through machine learning. However, despite the numerous research efforts, more efforts are needed for the establishment of an effective database for CDs synthesis. Moreover, it is currently implemented mainly to assist in the prediction of fluorescence properties of CDs through machine learning, and has not achieved the prediction of electrochemical properties. In the field of batteries this has not yet taken off, and there are great and unlimited possibilities.
